peer_exemplars.reasoning: High openness and strong anti-domination values drive support for transparency, but moderate trust in institutions and a libertarian skepticism of state control temper enthusiasm for mandated disclosure.

High libertarianism and anti-censorship orientation, combined with high system justification and trust in institutions, lead to moderate support for transparency as a check on power without heavy regulation.

High extraversion and low agreeableness (HEXACO) combined with strong anti-elitism and distrust of institutions (trust in science at 2.57) produce moderate support for transparency as a check on opaque power, but low trust in government (7.77) tempers enthusiasm for mandated disclosure.

Her very high openness to experience (99.47%), very high agreeableness (97.69%), strong libertarian anti-censorship orientation (90.53%), and populist anti-elitism (71.46%) combine to support forcing opaque algorithms into the open, while her moderate trust in government (12.56%) tempers the score from maximal.

Extreme authoritarianism (94.85%) and high conventionalism (96.59%) drive strong opposition to algorithmic transparency as a threat to orderly information control, reinforced by low trust in science and experts (21.73%) and moderate digital confidence.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: A dangerous-worldview and high threat sensitivity make inauthentic behavior a salient concern, while high civic nationalism and fact-checking behavior support disclosure, though low conscientiousness limits the push for strict enforcement.

Strong fairness and reciprocity moral foundation, along with high honesty-humility, drive support for disclosure to protect integrity, though moderate authoritarianism tempers enthusiasm for punitive measures.

Very high extraversion and high conscientiousness (Big Five) alongside a dangerous worldview (71.59) and strong nationalism (ethnic nationalism 92.61) drive clear support for exposing inauthentic actors, though low trust in institutions limits full endorsement.

Her high HEXACO honesty-humility (80.25%), moral-fairness/reciprocity (89.93%), and authoritarian law-and-order preference (79.87%) drive support for disclosure, but her very low political trust (22.19%) and moderate competitive-jungle worldview (51.52%) keep it from being a maximal score.

Combined high social dominance orientation (72.5%) and strong collective narcissism (94.25%) lead to rejection of bot disclosure mandates as unnecessary interference with dominant group information flows, despite moderate internal political efficacy.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: High trust in government and political institutions, combined with moderate system justification, leads to clear disagreement with the notion of government distrust of elections, though a competitive-jungle worldview adds a slight edge of cynicism.
High system justification and trust in institutions, along with low populism, lead to mild disagreement with distrust, but low political efficacy and high threat sensitivity create some ambivalence.

Extremely low trust in government (7.77) and judiciary (6.29) combined with high anti-elitism (52.77), a competitive worldview (87.64), and strong ethnic nationalism (92.61) produce very strong distrust of electoral processes, viewing them as rigged by elites.

Her very low trust in government (12.56%) and strong populist anti-elitism (71.46%) would suggest some distrust, but her very high agreeableness (97.69%), high conscientiousness-dutifulness (82.54%), and strong civic nationalism (80.18%) push her toward institutional confidence, resulting in a mild negative score reflecting skepticism but not opposition.

Very high system justification (77.01%) and conventionalism (96.59%) combined with strong authority deference (75.34%) produce maximal opposition to election distrust, as such distrust fundamentally threatens traditional institutional legitimacy.
